content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 25, 1991

Robert H. Cornell
City Attorney
Town of Colma
525 Market Street, Suite 3700
San Francisco, CA  94105-2745

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-212

Dear Mr. Cornell:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Colma City Councilmembers Dennis Fiscaro and Ray
Boudewyn regarding their responsibilities under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Since your advice request does not refer to a specific
governmental decision but seeks general guidance, we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

As members of the Colma City Council and volunteer firemen
with the Colma Fire Protection District, may Councilmembers
Fiscaro and Boudewyn participate in city council decisions which
may affect the Colma Fire Protection District?

CONCLUSION

The Colma Fire Protection District appears to be a public
entity, and as such, is not a "business entity" as defined in the
Act.  Further, salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem

___________________________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-212
Page 2


received from a state, local or federal government agency is
expressly exempted from the definition of "income" for purposes of
the Act. Consequently, since the payments the councilmembers
receive are in the form of governmental salary, the payments would
not create a conflict of interest with respect to decisions
affecting the Colma Fire Protection District.

FACTS

Councilmembers Dennis Fisicaro and Ray Boudewyn are incumbent
city councilmembers for the Town of Colma and are also volunteer
firemen for the Colma Fire Protection District (the "district").
The city council may consider in the future the following
decisions: (1) whether or not the Town of Colma should stay
within the district or whether the town should establish an
independent city fire department or possibly contract for fire
protection services from an alternate source; and, (2) if the town
stays within the district, the amount of cash contributions the
town should make to the district.

Since the councilmembers are volunteers with the district,
you have become concerned that they may have a conflict of
interest with respect to decisions that affect the district. You
stated that the district is a separate entity which encompasses
unincorporated Colma and other unincorporated areas of San Mateo
County. The fire district is controlled by an elected commission
and is manned by volunteers. The fire-fighter volunteers receive
no salary but do receive compensation for each actual call. There
are some additional minor benefits, such as dinners at the fire
house.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. A "public official" is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural
person who is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a
state or local government agency. This definition would include
members of the Colma City Council.

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,

Our File No. I-91-212
Page 3


     received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

                    Section 87103(c) and (d).

     In your letter you stated that two of your city
councilmembers are "employed" as volunteer firefighters with the
Colma Fire Protection District. While these councilmembers are
not considered regular "employees" of the district, they do
receive compensation for each call. Moreover, they receive meals
at the firehouse while on call. We assume that they have received
more than $250 from the district in the past 12 months.

     However, the district is a public entity with an elected
board of directors. Local government agencies are not
organizations or enterprises operated for profit, and therefore
are not business entities under the Act. Thus, your
councilmembers' position with the district will not create a
conflict of interest concerning decisions affecting it pursuant to
Section 87103(d). (Section 82005; Darcy Advice Letter, No. I-87-
296.)

     Further, salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem
received from a state, local or federal government agency is
expressly exempted from the definition of "income" for purposes of
the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).) Thus, if the payments received
by the councilmembers are in the form of salary, they would not
create a conflict of interest with respect to decisions affecting
the district. (Section 87103(c).)

     You stated that the councilmembers receive compensation as
volunteer firemen for the district. They are paid for each call
they are on. While the payments are on a per-call basis, we
still believe the exception in Section 82030(b)(2) applies. In
the Jenke Advice Letter (No. A-83-001) which concerned employees
of a public hospital, we stated:

          The Hospital could employ its own full-time
     staff or arrange for physician services on a part-
     time or as-needed basis. In the case of a small
     hospital, the latter arrangement makes more sense
     as it permits greater diversity of services.
     Consequently, under the circumstances given here,
     we consider the payments which Jenke, Inc. receives
     for his work as Medical Director of the Nursing
     Home and for interpretive work at the Hospital to
     be salary from a local governmental entity, subject
     to the exclusion under subdivision (b)(2) of
     Section 82030.

Our File No. I-91-212
Page 4

Consistent with this line of reasoning we would conclude that
the payments the councilmembers receive as volunteer firemen would
be salary from a local government agency and excluded from the
definition of "income" under the Act.

Thus, absent some other disqualifying financial interest in a
decision as set forth in Section 87103, or some direct financial
effect on the councilmembers personally resulting from a
governmental decision, the councilmembers may participate in city
council decisions which may affect the district. (Sampson Advice
Letter, No. I-89-1964.) However, please note that our advice is
limited to the Political Reform Act. You may wish to contact the
Attorney General's office with respect to other provisions of the
law such as Government Code 1090 and the law governing
incompatible offices.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.³

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
     Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

_____________________________

³ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.facts:
Councilmembers Dennis Fisicaro and Ray Boudewyn are incumbent city councilmembers for the Town of Colma and are also volunteer firemen for the Colma Fire Protection District (the "district"). The city council may consider in the future the following decisions: (1) whether or not the Town of Colma should stay within the district or whether the town should establish an independent city fire department or possibly contract for fire protection services from an alternate source; and, (2) if the town stays within the district, the amount of cash contributions the town should make to the district.

Since the councilmembers are volunteers with the district, you have become concerned that they may have a conflict of interest with respect to decisions that affect the district. You stated that the district is a separate entity which encompasses unincorporated Colma and other unincorporated areas of San Mateo County. The Fire District is controlled by an elected commission and is manned by volunteers. The fire-fighter volunteers receive no salary but do receive compensation for each actual call. There are some additional minor benefits, such as dinners at the fire house.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A "public official" is defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency. This definition would include members of the Colma City Council.

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,

received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(c) and (d).

In your letter you stated that two of your city councilmembers are "employed" as volunteer firefighters with the Colma Fire Protection District. While these councilmembers are not considered regular "employees" of the district, they do receive compensation for each call. Moreover, they receive meals at the firehouse while on call. We assume that they have received more than $250 from the district in the past 12 months.

However, the district is a public entity with an elected board of directors. Local government agencies are not organizations or enterprises operated for profit, and therefore are not business entities under the Act. Thus, your councilmembers' position with the district will not create a conflict of interest concerning decisions affecting it pursuant to Section 87103(d). (Section 82005; Darcy Advice Letter, No. I-87-296.)

Further, salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state, local or federal government agency is expressly exempted from the definition of "income" for purposes of the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).) Thus, if the payments received by the councilmembers are in the form of salary, they would not create a conflict of interest with respect to decisions affecting the district. (Section 87103(c).)

You stated that the councilmembers receive compensation as volunteer firemen for the district. They are paid for each call they are on. While the payments are on a piece-meal basis, we still believe the exception in Section 82030(b)(2) applies. In the Jenke Advice Letter (No. A-83-001) which concerned employees of a public hospital, we stated:

The Hospital could employ its own full-time staff or arrange for physician services on a part-time or as-needed basis. In the case of a small hospital, the latter arrangement makes more sense as it permits greater diversity of services. Consequently, under the circumstances given here, we consider the payments which Jenke, Inc. receives for his work as Medical Director of the Nursing Home and for interpretive work at the Hospital to be salary from a local governmental entity, subject to the exclusion under subdivision (b)(2) of Section 82030.

Consistent with this line of reasoning we would conclude that the payments the councilmembers receive as volunteer firemen would be salary from a local government agency and excluded from the definition of "income" under the Act.

Thus, absent some other disqualifying financial interest in a decision as set forth in Section 87103, or some direct financial effect on the councilmembers personally resulting from a governmental decision, the councilmembers may participate in city council decisions which may affect the district. (Sampson Advice Letter, No. I-89-196.) However, please note that our advice is limited to the Political Reform Act. You may wish to contact the Attorney General's office with respect to other provisions of the law such as Government Code 1090 and the law governing incompatible offices.
